title: Remarks: A Reset on Liquidity Regulation
summary: Under Secretary Jonathan McKernan outlined Treasury's case for reforming post-2008 liquidity regulation, arguing the current framework excessively constrains bank lending capacity. The speech cites March 2023 banking stress (SVB, Signature, First Republic) as evidence that the framework's assumptions about discount window access and buffer useability were flawed. McKernan proposes giving capped recognition to discount window borrowing capacity tied to prepositioned collateral as a near-term reform to restore the lender of last resort to its intended role.
key_points: Post-2008 liquidity regulation was designed to reduce dependence on the lender of last resort, but March 2023 demonstrated the framework's assumptions about buffer useability and discount window access were flawed

SVB, Signature, and First Republic held substantial Treasuries and agency MBS, but liquidity existed only on paper because collateral was not fully prepositioned and discount window access was untested
Currently, 25% of large banks' balance sheets are allocated to safe assets, up from roughly 10% before the crisis, constraining lending capacity
Proposed reform would give capped recognition to borrowing capacity from collateral prepositioned at the discount window, creating incentives for prepositioning and regular testing
Central bank borrowing would remain collateralized with conservative haircuts and limited to solvent institutions
Treasury connects liquidity reform to unlocking hundreds of billions to potentially trillions in new lending for AI infrastructure, domestic supply chains, and defense industrial base
why_it_matters: Liquidity regulation directly determines how much of the banking system balance sheet must be held in low-yielding safe assets versus deployed for productive lending. If implemented, these reforms could shift the boundary between bank self-insurance and central bank liquidity support, potentially freeing capital for lending but also changing how systemic liquidity stress might be transmitted. The March 2023 stress events exposed that buffers designed as shock absorbers were treated as untouchable minimums, exacerbating rather than mitigating stress transmission.
macro_context: The speech explicitly links liquidity reform to financing America's 'Golden Age' transformation, including AI infrastructure, onshoring of manufacturing, and the defense industrial base. Treasury argues the current framework 'excessively and unnecessarily' limits banks' ability to lend for these stated priorities.
market_structure_context: The speech addresses the structural relationship between bank liquidity regulation, discount window operations, and the lender of last resort function. It critiques the post-2008 framework for inadvertently: (1) treating liquidity buffers as hard minimums rather than usable shock absorbers, (2) entrenching discount window stigma by creating incentives to exhaust buffers before accessing the window, and (3) driving financial activity to nonbanks through capital arbitrage. The proposed reform framework would rebalance these structural incentives.
notes: This is a substantive policy speech from Treasury leadership outlining the case for liquidity regulatory reform. The speech is authored (As Prepared By: Secretary Scott Bessent) and delivered (As Delivered By: Under Secretary Jonathan McKernan) by senior Treasury officials at a roundtable on bank liquidity. The source contains specific factual claims about: (1) the evolution of bank balance sheet allocation post-2008, (2) the mechanics and limitations of the current liquidity coverage ratio framework, (3) the events of March 2023 involving SVB, Signature, and First Republic, and (4) proposed near-term regulatory reforms. No explicit forward-looking market impact statements are made. Confidence score lowered from higher value because the speech does not provide implementation timelines or specific quantitative estimates for the proposed reforms' effects on credit availability or asset prices.
